text: The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the liver, using instruments inserted through the trocars positioned in a linear fashion along the patient's right side.
The surgeon divides adhesions from a previous cholecystectomy, using instruments inserted through the robotic trocars.
The surgeon mobilizes the right triangular ligament and retroperitoneal attachments of the liver, using instruments through the trocars positioned on the patient's right side.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection of the liver until the inferior vena cava is visually encountered, indicating the progression of the procedure.
Wristed instruments are used to facilitate the dissection, allowing precise manipulation and separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to retract the adequately mobilized liver cephalad, exposing the anterior surface of the adrenal gland.
The fourth robotic arm is used to retract the liver, eliminating the need for an assistant, and providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the adrenal gland by opening Girauda's fascia at the superior medial aspect of the kidney, using wristed instruments for precise manipulation.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection, removing retroperitoneal fat from the superior aspect of the kidney, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
During the dissection, the surgeon injects four cc's of indocyanine green solution intravenously, which may enhance visualization of vascular structures.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to identify the inferior border of the adrenal gland, highlighting the structure with fluorescence.
The surgeon identifies the border of the adrenal gland, ensuring precise dissection to avoid entering the gland itself, which could lead to bleeding and complicate the procedure.
The surgeon continues dissection posterior to the adrenal gland, revealing the underlying psoas muscle.
The surgeon clears retroperitoneal fat from the psoas muscle, incorporating it into the adrenal gland specimen.

The surgeon uses the left hand to retract the adrenal gland and retroperitoneal fat anteriorly, placing the retroperitoneal fat along the medial border of the adrenal gland under tension to facilitate dissection.
The surgeon opens Girota's fascia medially to initiate the medial dissection of the adrenal gland.
The robotic camera provides enhanced visualization during the medial dissection, allowing the surgeon to divide arterial vessels and identify the adrenal vein adjacent to the adrenal gland.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to identify the inferior medial border of the adrenal gland, preparing for the dissection of the retroperitoneal fat at the gland's medial aspect.
The surgeon carefully dissects the medial tissue surrounding the adrenal gland, ensuring the gland itself is not violated to prevent unnecessary bleeding.

The surgeon uses improved visualization from the robotic camera to identify a safe location for retracting the adrenal gland without directly grasping it, ensuring minimal risk of damage to the gland.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system intermittently during the medial dissection to visually identify the border of the adrenal gland and assess for the presence of the adrenal vein.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection superiorly, clearing retroperitoneal fat from the surface of the psoas muscle.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide small arterial vessels during the dissection of the retroperitoneal fat at the medial aspect of the adrenal gland.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to identify the medial border of the adrenal gland and assess the presence of the adrenal vein, ensuring precise dissection without damaging the gland.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection, following the inferior vena cava in a cephalad direction, carefully navigating around the surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a wristed instrument in the right hand to perform the medial dissection, carefully maneuvering around the inferior vena cava without significant manipulation of the vessel.
The Firefly imaging technique highlights the adrenal vein, making it clearly identifiable against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects the retroperitoneal fat at the medial aspect of the adrenal gland, progressively revealing the adrenal vein.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to circumferentially dissect the adrenal vein, which is clearly visualized with the aid of Firefly imaging.
The surgeon clips the adrenal vein and then divides it, completing the dissection process.
The surgeon uses wristed instruments to achieve optimal angles for clipping and dividing the adrenal vein, enhancing precision in the dissection process.
The surgeon continues dissection along the superior aspect of the adrenal gland, carefully separating surrounding tissues to expose the gland's surface.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to identify the superior border of the adrenal gland, enhancing visualization of the anatomical structure.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection, clearing retroperitoneal fat from the surface of the psoas muscle.
The surgeon clears the retroperitoneal fat from the superior aspect of the kidney, moving inferiorly.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection to the level of the peritoneal reflection, clearing tissue in this area.
The surgeon completes the procedure by clearing retroperitoneal fat from the psoas muscle and the superior aspect of the kidney, ensuring these areas are free of fatty tissue.
The dissection extends laterally to the peritoneal reflection and medially towards the inferior vena cava, clearing retroperitoneal fat in these areas.
